questions.question: Why does early romantic love feel obsessive and all-consuming (constant thoughts about partner, difficulty focusing on other activities)?

options.text: Because you have a mental disorder
Because reduced serotonin during early love (similar to OCD patterns) combined with dopamine-driven reward seeking creates obsessive thinking — this normalizes after 12-18 months
Because early love has no biological basis — it's purely psychological
Obsessive thinking is abnormal and means the relationship is unhealthy

questions.explanation: Research shows reduced serotonin during early romantic love, similar to patterns seen in OCD. Low serotonin correlates with obsessive, intrusive thinking. Combined with high dopamine (reward seeking), this creates the 'I can't stop thinking about them' experience. This is biologically normal and temporary — serotonin normalizes after 12-18 months as the relationship transitions from novelty-seeking to attachment bonding. Obsessive quality fading is healthy, not a loss of love.

questions.question: What brain changes explain why people in early love often ignore red flags or make risky decisions?

options.text: No brain changes — people are just being careless
Reduced prefrontal cortex activity (rational judgment) and reduced amygdala activity (fear/threat detection) — adaptive for bonding but risky for decision-making
Increased prefrontal cortex activity makes people hyper-rational
Early love has no effect on judgment

questions.explanation: fMRI studies show REDUCED prefrontal cortex (critical evaluation, rational decision-making) and REDUCED amygdala (fear processing, threat detection) activity during early love. This creates 'rose-colored glasses' (idealization, not seeing flaws) and reduced fear around the beloved. This is adaptive for bonding (facilitates attachment despite risk) but dangerous when it obscures genuine red flags (abuse, coercive control, fundamental incompatibility). Biology explains the phenomenon but doesn't justify ignoring safety concerns.

questions.question: What is the typical timeline for early-love intensity (obsessive thinking, constant euphoria) to transition to long-term bonding?

options.text: Intensity should last forever in 'true love'
Peak intensity 0-6 months; gradual normalization 6-18 months; transition to long-term attachment bonding 18-24 months — with significant individual variation
Exactly 6 months for all relationships
There is no transition — early intensity either continues or the relationship fails

questions.explanation: Research suggests typical timeline: 0-6 months peak dopamine/norepinephrine, reduced serotonin (obsessive intensity); 6-18 months gradual serotonin normalization; 18-24 months transition to long-term attachment bonding (dopamine + oxytocin/opioid). IMPORTANT: individual variation is enormous — some faster, some slower. Relationship quality, external stress, and individual differences affect timeline. This is an average pattern, not a rigid formula. Expecting permanent intensity is unrealistic.

questions.question: What red flags should NOT be dismissed even when early love intensity feels overwhelming?

options.text: All relationship concerns are just overthinking — early intensity proves the relationship is right

Isolation from friends/family, controlling behavior (monitoring phone/finances), rapid escalation to major commitments, explosive anger or jealousy — these require safety assessment regardless of chemistry
Any disagreement or conflict is a red flag
Red flags are irrelevant if you feel dopamine-driven attraction

questions.explanation: Early love biology (reduced judgment, intense focus) can obscure warning signs of abuse or coercive control. Red flags that should NEVER be dismissed: partner isolates you from support networks, monitors/controls your activities or finances, pushes for rapid major commitments (moving in/marriage within weeks), shows explosive anger/jealousy, or creates fear. These are not 'just early intensity' — they're safety concerns requiring professional assessment (National DV Hotline: 1-800-799-7233). Intense chemistry does NOT validate unsafe behavior.

questions.question: Which statement accurately reflects the evidence on early romantic love intensity?

options.text: Early intensity predicts long-term relationship success
Early intensity is dopamine-driven novelty-seeking (temporary, biologically normal) — what predicts long-term success is communication, conflict management, values alignment, and attachment security
Absence of early intensity means the relationship is doomed
Early intensity should guide all major relationship decisions

questions.explanation: Research shows initial passion intensity is a WEAK predictor of long-term satisfaction. What matters more: communication quality, conflict management skills, values alignment, attachment security, and life stress management. Early intensity is pleasant but doesn't determine longevity. Some people (especially those with higher attachment avoidance or trauma) don't experience intense early infatuation even in healthy relationships. Absence of obsessive intensity ≠ absence of love. Major decisions should balance chemistry with rational assessment of compatibility.

title: Early Love — Dopamine and the Reward System - Quiz
